dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, dispatch to 450 Lee Street for a 58-year-old male with difficulty breathing.
Hi, I'm Morgan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
He's been wheezing and struggling to breathe for about 30 minutes or so.
What is your name? When is your birthday?
Martin, uh October 28, 1962.
What county are we in?
[CITY], I think.
What happened today?
I was just watching TV when it got really bad. I tried to get help from the girls here, but I asked for an ambulance.
Can we check your breathing and airway? I'll take a quick listen first.
Airway is patent, no signs of obstruction.
We heard audible wheezing before we got to you, Mr. Martin.
I'll take some quick breathing vitals now, partner.
Respirations are at 26 per minute, clearly fast and wheezing.
Pulse is 110, definitely tachycardic.
Breathing is wheezy, with accessory muscles used heavily in the neck.
Breathing is noted to be wheezy, both inspiratory and expiratory in both lung fields.
Chest rise is equal and symmetric, but he's definitely using quite a few accessory muscles.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia and no signs of ischemia or arrhythmia.
Pulse ox being recorded at 100% on room air.
Glucose is currently reading 0, we'll note it and avoid testing without further clearance from the ED.
Blood pressure is 138/99 via right arm assessment.
Mr. Martin, can you tell me the date today?
October 2, 2020.
What is your name?
Martin.
Where are we?
[CITY], [STATE].
Bystander, can you share how Mr. Martin got this bad?
He was watching TV, and it started suddenly. I tried to help him drive to the hospital, but I couldn't get him there.
Okay, and any past surgeries or trauma that might relate?
Oh, I had a gunshot wound before, a while back. It required a trach in the hospital, but nothing here in the field.
Do you have a history of responding to steroids during episodes like this one?
Yeah, they usually have to give it to me when it gets this bad.
Skin is normal, warm, dry, and intact. No rashes or bite marks noted.
Head and neck are free of pain or tenderness.
Pupils are equal and reactive at approximately 4mm.
What medications do you currently take?
I use albuterol for my breathing and dapsone for another condition.
What was the last thing you had to eat or drink?
An hour or so before this, I had a sandwich and a glass of water.
You're in acute respiratory distress. Breathing sounds show inspiratory and expiratory wheezing in all areas.
No chest pain or tightness reported at this time.
Limbs are mobile with normal movement. Prior to our arrival, you were ambulatory, but we are not allowing further movement due to risk.
Pelvis is stable, no pain or deformity.
Neck and back appear normal with no DCAP-BTLS findings.
Medical records show a history of IV drug use, including heroin and crack cocaine.
Has this drug use occurred recently?
No, it's been in the past, not lately.
We saw a capped needle in your pocket labeled insulin-do you have diabetes?
No, I don't. I think it's something else, maybe.
Any known drug allergies?
No, no known drug allergies.
Peripheral pulses are noted as strong and equal in all extremities.
Have you ever had a field EMS intubation?
No, only in the hospital for the trach a while back.
You mentioned past health issues. Any diagnoses like COPD or chronic bronchitis?
Yes, I have COPD and chronic bronchitis. And anxiety too.
We are giving you a nebulizer treatment with a combination of albuterol and Atrovent, as this is a common and effective treatment for your wheezing.
Albuterol is 5 mg, Atrovent is 0.5 mg, through a nebulizer with a filter in place.
Also, you'll receive terbutaline subcutaneously to help widen your airway.
We're applying oxygen via nasal cannula at 6 liters per minute to support your breathing.
Capnography is in place now, and we've placed you on a four-lead EKG for continuous monitoring.
We respect your choice to not take an IV at this time-it will be noted and revisited if needed.
Pulse remains at 110, respiration is 26, and blood pressure still 138/99.
Breath sounds remain wheezy but oxygen saturation stays at 100%.
You look noticeably more comfortable now, so let's prepare to move you to the stretcher for further stabilizing.
Stretcher is on its way. When you're ready, we'll safely transfer you into the ambulance for rapid transport.
Roll your shoulders back a bit to let the stretcher come up. That's perfect, all set.
Martin, stay as calm as you can and continue with slow, steady breaths.
I'm doing okay, but I'm not going to pretend I don't need this.
We are heading directly to [COMPANY_NAME], as per protocol, for emergency evaluation and care.
Partner, let's move the stretcher in now. Side door closing-ready for departure.
We are underway to [COMPANY_NAME] now. They've been informed and are awaiting our arrival.
Keep using the nebulizer and your oxygen. You're in great hands, right here with us.
We're rolling into the hospital now and preparing to transfer you. If you notice any change, let us know.
The patient had a capped syringe labeled insulin found in their pocket during transport-an important note for the ED team.
ED contact has been made in transit with a critical report ready for the ER team.
We're now approaching the ER entrance. Let's complete a smooth and safe transfer.
Wheel him in directly onto the ER bed. The team is standing by and expects a high acuity patient.
That's all for the transfer. Let them know we're here, and all notes and vitals are complete.
We note you're not experiencing any pain at this time, which is an important observation to include in the medical summary.
